title: Dark gingerbread loaf
subtitle: The sticky, treacle-dark loaf that improves with keeping
excerpt: A 900 g gingerbread loaf made with black treacle and dark muscovado sugar, ground ginger, mixed spice, and a long slow bake at 160°C. The loaf is almost too soft on the day it is baked; by the second day the crumb has set and the ginger is deeper. Keeps for a week.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the gingerbread chapter documents several forms of the bake — crisp ginger biscuits, soft gingerbread parkin, and the dark treacle-based loaf. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: Acton's gingerbread entries use black treacle and ground ginger as the primary flavour agents. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): Yorkshire parkin and regional gingerbread traditions across the North of England are documented, all centring on black treacle and oatmeal or flour. The baker's percentage approach is used for flour weight; the recipe here follows the standard British dark gingerbread ratio of approximately 1:1 flour to treacle-plus-sugar by weight.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 loaf (900 g / 2 lb tin, cuts to 10 slices)
recipe.freezeNotes: Slice and freeze; the treacle content means the crumb survives freezing well. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best after 24 hours. Keeps wrapped at room temperature for up to a week, becoming more moist and intensely gingery with each day.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf — low and slow to prevent the treacle from burning

recipeTools.notes: 900 g (2 lb) tin, greased and lined.

glossaryTerms.definition: A cake-mixing technique where the fat (butter, treacle, golden syrup) is melted with the sugar over gentle heat, then cooled before adding eggs and dry ingredients. Used for dense, sticky cakes where creaming would produce the wrong texture. The melted mixture should be at room temperature, not hot, before the eggs go in.

content.text: Dark gingerbread is the opposite of a light sponge. The black treacle and dark muscovado sugar carry a bitterness that the ground ginger and mixed spice balance; the result on the day of baking is almost too intense, too moist, slightly heavy. But by day two the crumb has set and the flavour has deepened and everything is in proportion. A week-old slice, kept wrapped at room temperature, is better than the freshly baked loaf. This is a cake to plan for.

: the treacle, sugar, and butter melt together in a saucepan, then cool before the eggs and dry ingredients are folded in. It is important that the melted mixture cools sufficiently — if the eggs go in while the mixture is hot, they scramble. The loaf bakes at 160°C fan, low enough that the treacle doesn't burn before the centre is cooked through.

items.prepNote: 3 teaspoons — assertive; reduce to 2 for a milder heat

content.text: Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g (2 lb) loaf tin. Measure the {{unsalted-butter}}, {{black-treacle}}, {{muscovado-dark}}, and {{whole-milk}} into a medium saucepan. Heat over low heat, stirring occasionally, until the butter has melted and the sugar has dissolved into the liquid. Do not boil. Pour into a large mixing bowl and leave to cool for at least 15 minutes. The mixture should be warm to the touch but not hot — when you can hold your palm against the bowl comfortably, it's ready for the eggs.

Beat the {{eggs}} eggs lightly and stir them into the cooled treacle mixture. Sift together the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{ginger-ground}}, {{mixed-spice}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} and fold them into the wet mixture in two additions. Fold gently with a spatula (about 10 folds per addition) until no dry flour remains. The batter will be quite runny; this is correct for a gingerbread loaf.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Bake for 55–65 minutes. The loaf is done when it feels firm when pressed in the centre and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. The top will be very dark — almost black in places where the treacle caramelises; this is expected and not burning. If the top cracks early (before 30 minutes), reduce the heat by 10°C and continue baking. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes before turning out. Leave on a rack until completely cold before wrapping.
